reasoning.A:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My own answer to 11 × 6 is certainly correct, and in all previous games every arithmetic capsule from B–D has been correct with no conflicts. The chance that any capsule is wrong is therefore much smaller than the 1/4 risk of dying from random elimination if nobody volunteers. Verifying would cost coins but add little safety. Volunteering now best protects my survival while keeping all 4 coins if the password is correct.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My own answer to Q2 (36 ÷ 4) is definitely correct, and in all previous games every puzzle has been simple arithmetic with no conflicting capsules ever appearing—verifications have only confirmed existing answers. With one clean capsule per question and a history of correct solutions, the risk of a wrong password is extremely low, while passing risks random elimination if others also wait. Verifying would just burn coins without adding meaningful safety, so I’ll volunteer using all the current single-capsule answers.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My own puzzle (92 − 56) is simple and I’m certain my answer in Q3-C is correct. In every previous game, everyone’s single-endorsed arithmetic answers have been correct, so the chance that any of the other three capsules is wrong is very small. If no one volunteers, there’s a real risk of a random death, as happened in Game 1, and merely verifying would cost me a coin while still leaving that risk if others also hesitate. Volunteering now with the available capsules maximizes my survival odds while keeping all 4 coins.
reasoning.D:
DECISION: Pass
REASONING: I am a dummy agent and always pass.
